## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BZ
2	
3	## Boundary — shape, not mechanism (cinched)
4	
5	This heat builds the **citizen tier** — the current keyfile, no-org operator-credential tier —
6	restructured so its capability layer is identical to the future federation tier's. The backing
7	stays keyfile; a no-org operator runs it unchanged. We do **not** touch federation here.
8	OUT of scope: Workforce pool/provider setup, STS exchange, IdP device flow, RBRD mode-enum
9	activation — federation's own future work (RBSHR "Operator federation"; memo-20260527).
10	
11	The payoff is structural, not nominal: the **capability layer** (capability-sets, grant/revoke,
12	the Terrier, the audit) becomes tier-blind, so a later federation switch reshapes nothing
13	in it — it swaps identity-kind (citizen → federate) and token mint (signed-JWT → STS) and nothing
14	else.
15	
16	Mechanism for everything below lives in `Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md`; this
17	paddock holds shape only.
18	
19	NOTE — supersedes memo-20260527: it says operator verbs "keep their names, only bodies differ" and
20	keyfile is "one SA key file each per role." This heat converges the verb *bodies* in shape, makes
21	the citizen tier one SA *per person*, and generalizes 20260527's role-keyed token seam to an
22	identity-keyed one. (A deferral-honoring breadcrumb is on memo-20260527 now; full note when frozen.)
23	
24	## The citizen model — identity decoupled from capability
25	
26	- **Citizen** = a keyfile-tier operator identity (governor/director/retriever), rank-neutral: rank
27	  is not a property of the citizen, it lives in the capability-sets it holds. Multi-role is
28	  first-class — one citizen holds several capability-sets. (Federation peer = *federate*; payor =
29	  founder, outside both. Definitions: memo.)
30	- **Identity lifecycle is decoupled from capability lifecycle** — the spine. Adding/removing a
31	  citizen is independent of granting/revoking its capabilities. Today's `invest` fuses the two;
32	  the model unfuses them. (Why this is achievable: memo.)
33	- **Capability-set** = a named bundle of IAM grants, defined in code (lifted out of the invest
34	  bodies). Tier-blind.
35	- **The verbs dissolve** to add-citizen / remove-citizen / grant / revoke / rekey + read-Terrier,
36	  parameterized by (actor, capability-set, citizen). invest/divest/mantle/roster stop existing as
37	  distinct concepts.
38	- **Capability verbs are tier-blind; only identity verbs branch by tier** (and by the payor's
39	  OAuth backing). rekey is keyfile-only.
40	- **Grant/revoke operate only on named capability-sets, never raw bindings** — the contamination
41	  guard that keeps the Terrier auditable.
42	- Ontology: the depot's civic ontology covers **operator** identities — citizens (and later
43	  federates), people who hold capabilities — and **holdings** (arks/hoards/bullions), property.
44	  Cloud-native system identities (Mason, future Envoy) sit outside it. ("citizen" supersedes
45	  RBSHR's prose use of it for an artifact, now reworded to "holding.")
46	
47	## Naming & the home — Manor, Terrier (settled this session)
48	
49	The Terrier's "home" is satisfied without inventing a polity abstraction (a framing
50	explored and dropped):
51	
52	- **Manor = the Payor project — concrete, singular, one-of.** The administrative seat: the one GCP
53	  project hosting the Payor SA, OAuth client, and billing, under which Depots are funded. NOT a
54	  cross-cloud polity. Now a first-class RBS0 quoin (`rbtgi_manor`, cult name of the former
55	  `rbtgi_payor_project`), its singularity made explicit so it stops being forgotten.
56	- **Terrier = the citizen-roll** — the depot-resident record of which citizen holds which
57	  capability-set (the stored intent, bonded to *citizen*). The **Manor hosts the Terrier** —
58	  resolving where the registry lives: the Payor project, where Terrier-write authority already
59	  sits. Recording AWS-targeting intent in the Terrier is not administering AWS (asymmetric
60	  sovereignty intact; Manor stays GCP-only). No grand polity concept unless multi-tenancy /
61	  multi-Manor becomes real.
62	
63	Reserved civic-ontology quoins — named here, NOT yet in RBS0 (premature pre-MVP): **Terrier**
64	(the citizen-roll), **Citizen** (operator-altitude identity, not a role), **capability-set** (cult name TBD —
65	portfolio/office). A new RBS0 category prefix likely houses the civic ontology when incorporated.
66	
67	Verb dispositions (gestalt-adjust where the word's soul survives; before MVP, no legacy — the gate
68	is semantic fit, not legacy cost):
69	
70	- **invest/divest → adjust in place** to capability grant/revoke (role-keyed `rbtgo_*_invest`
71	  quoins collapse to one role-generic verb).
72	- **charter/knight → retire** (role-bound). **roster → retire** (dissolves into reading the
73	  Terrier). **mantle → TBD.**
74	- **membership (add/remove citizen) → mint** civic verbs: **enfranchise / expel**.
75	
76	## Intent vs enforcement — the Terrier (cinched)
77	
78	Enforcement is server-side IAM, always. Intent — which citizen is meant to hold which capability —
79	is stored in the depot-resident **Terrier**, distinct from IAM. The audit is the diff between
80	**actual IAM state** and the Terrier; the routine Terrier read is identical across tiers.
81	Federation forces the same Terrier anyway, so it is paid once for both. (Today's name-regex `roster`
82	verb is retired — no prefix to filter on. Why it must be stored, and where it lives: memo.)
83	
84	## The audit — drift as signal, asymmetric healing (cinched)
85	
86	The audit measures drift between declared intent (the Terrier) and IAM. Healing is asymmetric, and the
87	asymmetry is the Pale recognition test:
88	
89	- **Deficit** (IAM short of intent — our own grant failed) is ours → auto-converge, logged.
90	- **Surplus** (IAM beyond intent — touched outside our verbs) crossed the Pale → report; a human
91	  adjudicates. The audit never auto-revokes; surplus resolution is human-driven.
92	
93	Both capability directions write intent first — grant writes the Terrier before adding bindings,
94	revoke withdraws it before removing them — so a crash lands as a safe report-only surplus, never an
95	auto-converging deficit. Auto-converge is safe only where Terrier-write authority is at least as
96	protected as grant (else a softer escalation path; see Open). And the audit reads **both
97	by-identity and by-resource-member**, so a grant to a principal absent from the Terrier cannot hide. (Drift
98	taxonomy, the member-first axis, definition-change gating, concurrency, and the orphan marker:
99	memo.)
100	
101	## Authority topology — the one seam with policy content
102	
103	"Who may grant which capability-set." Shape:
104	
105	- Authority boundaries are IAM-real only where they fall on resource-scope splits (payor/governor
106	  holds because billing is a separate resource; governor/director does not).
107	- Grant authority is preventively scopable at **project** scope but only **detectively** at
108	  **resource** scope — so the resolution composes: IAM-limit the project-scoped grants,
109	  detective-audit the resource-scoped residual, and pull authority to the payor where a standing
110	  governor buys nothing.
111	- **Tier-dependent**: keyfile/solo → topology (no/minimal standing governor); federation/multi-admin
112	  → a condition-limited governor. The model allows the answer to differ (actor and capability-set
113	  are parameters).
114	- Surplus-detection's security value depends on the topology — do not substitute detective audit for
115	  structural closure. (The IAM attribute, its scope/role limits, and the seam reasoning: memo.)
116	
117	## Blast radius — larger resting target, finer response (cinched)
118	
119	One citizen = one SA = one key carrying the union of held capabilities — forced by "one identity per
120	person," accepted as a tradeoff. The resting-key form of this exposure is citizen-tier-only
121	(federation has no key at rest; the union property itself is tier-blind); most citizens wear one
122	hat; and decoupling buys finer incident response (revoke a capability without touching the key;
123	rekey without touching capabilities). (Reasoning: memo.)
124	
125	## Inherited concern — governor teardown leak
126	
127	Governor teardown sits inside this heat's blast radius: today's mantle leaks a project tombstone
128	per re-mantle. The convergence closes it (cheap revoke-before-delete; an idempotent governor is a
129	further, not-free refinement). Mechanism + standalone fallback if descoped:
130	memo + memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md.
131	
132	## What done looks like
133	
134	Every cult-verb surface migrated to the citizen/capability vocabulary: workstation bash (role-keyed
135	selection collapses to one identity accessor), the governor/payor admin verbs (the admin verbs
136	collapse to the dissolved set), cloud-side bash naming roles, the .adoc specs voicing the cult
137	verbs, the Rust theurge cases, the onboarding handbook. "citizen" replaces the role-named identity
138	throughout, and the SA name itself becomes identity-only (the naming migration: memo).
139	
140	The load-bearing federation-enabling core is narrow: route the role/file-path-keyed token-accessor
141	calls through one identity-keyed accessor (generalizing memo-20260527's "single code seam"). The
142	vocabulary convergence is the larger, motivated-but-not-required remainder. Scoping/heat-chopping is
143	deferred — recorded here as shape.
144	
145	## Cinched decisions
146	
147	- Enforcement is server-side IAM. No file or client-side check is ever the boundary.
148	- The identity is the **citizen** — rank-neutral, keyfile-backed (federation peer *federate*; payor
149	  founder outside both). Identity lifecycle decoupled from capability lifecycle. Multi-role
150	  first-class.
151	- The citizen tier is one SA (one key, union of capabilities) per person, not one per role —
152	  blast-radius tradeoff accepted.
153	- Capability-sets are named code; grant/revoke operate only on named sets, never raw bindings.
154	- The Terrier (intent) is part of the model; IAM is enforcement; the audit diffs them.
155	- Audit healing is asymmetric: auto-converge deficits, report surplus; the audit never auto-revokes.
156	- Terrier-write authority is never a capability-set member; the Terrier writer for an entry is the
157	  grant-authority holder under the active topology.
158	- Governor breadth is resolved per tier, not globally: keyfile pulls grant authority to the payor /
159	  minimizes the standing governor; federation may keep a condition-limited governor.
160	- The mode enum (keyfile vs federation) belongs in RBRD and is added only when federation lands —
161	  not in this heat.
162	- Migration grandfathers existing role-named SAs: recover each citizen's role from the role-prefixed
163	  SA name (a one-time bootstrap, distinct from the retired standing "derive from IAM"), register
164	  citizens in the Terrier, let legacy names age out. No flag day.
165	- The convergence covers operator citizens; cloud-native identities (Mason, future Envoy) share the
166	  capability-set abstraction but not the citizen lifecycle.
167	
168	## Retired cinches
169	
170	- "Roster derives from IAM (an `rbk-identity` label), not a stored list — it cannot drift." Retired:
171	  under decoupling, deriving from IAM is lossy and makes the intended-vs-actual audit a tautology,
172	  and SA labels do not exist (SAs support tags, in Preview). Replaced by the Terrier +
173	  asymmetric audit. The true invariant it reached for — no enforcement state outside IAM — is
174	  preserved; the false corollary (no intent state may exist) is dropped.
175	
176	## Open — resolve within the heat
177	
178	- Where the Terrier physically lives — direction settled: hosted in the **Manor**
179	  (Payor project), where Terrier-write authority sits. Remaining: the exact object form (a separate
180	  access-restricted object vs a GAR artifact), explicitly **not** the build bucket (the director
181	  holds objectCreator there); write-ACL held only by governor/payor (the Terrier-write invariant;
182	  see The audit).
183	- Whether the keyfile tier drops the standing governor entirely or demotes it to non-privileged —
184	  this choice gates whether project-scope grant-limiting is in-heat.
185	- The citizen orphan marker: SA tags (Preview) vs description sentinel (both advisory; the
186	  authoritative orphan signal is a key on an SA absent from the Terrier — see memo).
187	- Whether to offer a combined add+grant convenience wrapper for the common one-hat citizen (the
188	  two-gesture flow itself follows from the decoupling).
189	
190	## Sources
191	
192	rbk-08-credential-repairs; memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races (the lifecycle
193	split — the enabler); memo-20260527-operator-credential-models (the two-tier plan and cult-verb
194	shape — breadcrumbed, full update when frozen); memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak
195	(standalone governor-leak fix); memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model (the mechanism this paddock
196	points to); memo-20260605-citizen-model-ultracode-process (how this revision was produced);
197	RBSHR "Operator federation". This revision folds in the 260605 design conversation and two review
198	passes (single-reviewer + ultracode multi-agent). The 260606 revision adds the Manor↔Census naming,
199	the first-class RBS0 Manor quoin (`rbtgi_manor`), and the verb dispositions above. The 260609
200	revision elects **Terrier** for the registry — retiring the C-initial *Census* (it collided with
201	Crucible) and dropping "ledger" as a competing name.

This heat builds the **citizen tier** — the current keyfile, no-org operator-credential tier —
restructured so its capability layer is identical to the future federation tier's. The backing
stays keyfile; a no-org operator runs it unchanged. We do **not** touch federation here.
OUT of scope: Workforce pool/provider setup, STS exchange, IdP device flow, RBRD mode-enum
activation — federation's own future work (RBSHR "Operator federation"; memo-20260527).

The payoff is structural, not nominal: the **capability layer** (capability-sets, grant/revoke,
the Terrier, the audit) becomes tier-blind, so a later federation switch reshapes nothing
in it — it swaps identity-kind (citizen → federate) and token mint (signed-JWT → STS) and nothing
else.

Mechanism for everything below lives in `Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md`; this
paddock holds shape only.

NOTE — supersedes memo-20260527: it says operator verbs "keep their names, only bodies differ" and
keyfile is "one SA key file each per role." This heat converges the verb *bodies* in shape, makes
the citizen tier one SA *per person*, and generalizes 20260527's role-keyed token seam to an
identity-keyed one. (A deferral-honoring breadcrumb is on memo-20260527 now; full note when frozen.)

## The citizen model — identity decoupled from capability

- **Citizen** = a keyfile-tier operator identity (governor/director/retriever), rank-neutral: rank
  is not a property of the citizen, it lives in the capability-sets it holds. Multi-role is
  first-class — one citizen holds several capability-sets. (Federation peer = *federate*; payor =
  founder, outside both. Definitions: memo.)
- **Identity lifecycle is decoupled from capability lifecycle** — the spine. Adding/removing a
  citizen is independent of granting/revoking its capabilities. Today's `invest` fuses the two;
  the model unfuses them. (Why this is achievable: memo.)
- **Capability-set** = a named bundle of IAM grants, defined in code (lifted out of the invest
  bodies). Tier-blind.
- **The verbs dissolve** to add-citizen / remove-citizen / grant / revoke / rekey + read-Terrier,
  parameterized by (actor, capability-set, citizen). invest/divest/mantle/roster stop existing as
  distinct concepts.
- **Capability verbs are tier-blind; only identity verbs branch by tier** (and by the payor's
  OAuth backing). rekey is keyfile-only.
- **Grant/revoke operate only on named capability-sets, never raw bindings** — the contamination
  guard that keeps the Terrier auditable.
- Ontology: the depot's civic ontology covers **operator** identities — citizens (and later
  federates), people who hold capabilities — and **holdings** (arks/hoards/bullions), property.
  Cloud-native system identities (Mason, future Envoy) sit outside it. ("citizen" supersedes
  RBSHR's prose use of it for an artifact, now reworded to "holding.")

## Naming & the home — Manor, Terrier (settled this session)

The Terrier's "home" is satisfied without inventing a polity abstraction (a framing
explored and dropped):

- **Manor = the Payor project — concrete, singular, one-of.** The administrative seat: the one GCP
  project hosting the Payor SA, OAuth client, and billing, under which Depots are funded. NOT a
  cross-cloud polity. Now a first-class RBS0 quoin (`rbtgi_manor`, cult name of the former
  `rbtgi_payor_project`), its singularity made explicit so it stops being forgotten.
- **Terrier = the citizen-roll** — the depot-resident record of which citizen holds which
  capability-set (the stored intent, bonded to *citizen*). The **Manor hosts the Terrier** —
  resolving where the registry lives: the Payor project, where Terrier-write authority already
  sits. Recording AWS-targeting intent in the Terrier is not administering AWS (asymmetric
  sovereignty intact; Manor stays GCP-only). No grand polity concept unless multi-tenancy /
  multi-Manor becomes real.

Reserved civic-ontology quoins — named here, NOT yet in RBS0 (premature pre-MVP): **Terrier**
(the citizen-roll), **Citizen** (operator-altitude identity, not a role), **capability-set** (cult name TBD —
portfolio/office). A new RBS0 category prefix likely houses the civic ontology when incorporated.

Verb dispositions (gestalt-adjust where the word's soul survives; before MVP, no legacy — the gate
is semantic fit, not legacy cost):

- **invest/divest → adjust in place** to capability grant/revoke (role-keyed `rbtgo_*_invest`
  quoins collapse to one role-generic verb).
- **charter/knight → retire** (role-bound). **roster → retire** (dissolves into reading the
  Terrier). **mantle → TBD.**
- **membership (add/remove citizen) → mint** civic verbs: **enfranchise / expel**.

## Intent vs enforcement — the Terrier (cinched)

Enforcement is server-side IAM, always. Intent — which citizen is meant to hold which capability —
is stored in the depot-resident **Terrier**, distinct from IAM. The audit is the diff between
**actual IAM state** and the Terrier; the routine Terrier read is identical across tiers.
Federation forces the same Terrier anyway, so it is paid once for both. (Today's name-regex `roster`
verb is retired — no prefix to filter on. Why it must be stored, and where it lives: memo.)

## The audit — drift as signal, asymmetric healing (cinched)

The audit measures drift between declared intent (the Terrier) and IAM. Healing is asymmetric, and the
asymmetry is the Pale recognition test:

- **Deficit** (IAM short of intent — our own grant failed) is ours → auto-converge, logged.
- **Surplus** (IAM beyond intent — touched outside our verbs) crossed the Pale → report; a human
  adjudicates. The audit never auto-revokes; surplus resolution is human-driven.

Both capability directions write intent first — grant writes the Terrier before adding bindings,
revoke withdraws it before removing them — so a crash lands as a safe report-only surplus, never an
auto-converging deficit. Auto-converge is safe only where Terrier-write authority is at least as
protected as grant (else a softer escalation path; see Open). And the audit reads **both
by-identity and by-resource-member**, so a grant to a principal absent from the Terrier cannot hide. (Drift
taxonomy, the member-first axis, definition-change gating, concurrency, and the orphan marker:
memo.)

## Authority topology — the one seam with policy content

"Who may grant which capability-set." Shape:

- Authority boundaries are IAM-real only where they fall on resource-scope splits (payor/governor
  holds because billing is a separate resource; governor/director does not).
- Grant authority is preventively scopable at **project** scope but only **detectively** at
  **resource** scope — so the resolution composes: IAM-limit the project-scoped grants,
  detective-audit the resource-scoped residual, and pull authority to the payor where a standing
  governor buys nothing.
- **Tier-dependent**: keyfile/solo → topology (no/minimal standing governor); federation/multi-admin
  → a condition-limited governor. The model allows the answer to differ (actor and capability-set
  are parameters).
- Surplus-detection's security value depends on the topology — do not substitute detective audit for
  structural closure. (The IAM attribute, its scope/role limits, and the seam reasoning: memo.)

## Blast radius — larger resting target, finer response (cinched)

One citizen = one SA = one key carrying the union of held capabilities — forced by "one identity per
person," accepted as a tradeoff. The resting-key form of this exposure is citizen-tier-only
(federation has no key at rest; the union property itself is tier-blind); most citizens wear one
hat; and decoupling buys finer incident response (revoke a capability without touching the key;
rekey without touching capabilities). (Reasoning: memo.)

## Inherited concern — governor teardown leak

Governor teardown sits inside this heat's blast radius: today's mantle leaks a project tombstone
per re-mantle. The convergence closes it (cheap revoke-before-delete; an idempotent governor is a
further, not-free refinement). Mechanism + standalone fallback if descoped:
memo + memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md.

## What done looks like

Every cult-verb surface migrated to the citizen/capability vocabulary: workstation bash (role-keyed
selection collapses to one identity accessor), the governor/payor admin verbs (the admin verbs
collapse to the dissolved set), cloud-side bash naming roles, the .adoc specs voicing the cult
verbs, the Rust theurge cases, the onboarding handbook. "citizen" replaces the role-named identity
throughout, and the SA name itself becomes identity-only (the naming migration: memo).

The load-bearing federation-enabling core is narrow: route the role/file-path-keyed token-accessor
calls through one identity-keyed accessor (generalizing memo-20260527's "single code seam"). The
vocabulary convergence is the larger, motivated-but-not-required remainder. Scoping/heat-chopping is
deferred — recorded here as shape.

## Cinched decisions

- Enforcement is server-side IAM. No file or client-side check is ever the boundary.
- The identity is the **citizen** — rank-neutral, keyfile-backed (federation peer *federate*; payor
  founder outside both). Identity lifecycle decoupled from capability lifecycle. Multi-role
  first-class.
- The citizen tier is one SA (one key, union of capabilities) per person, not one per role —
  blast-radius tradeoff accepted.
- Capability-sets are named code; grant/revoke operate only on named sets, never raw bindings.
- The Terrier (intent) is part of the model; IAM is enforcement; the audit diffs them.
- Audit healing is asymmetric: auto-converge deficits, report surplus; the audit never auto-revokes.
- Terrier-write authority is never a capability-set member; the Terrier writer for an entry is the
  grant-authority holder under the active topology.
- Governor breadth is resolved per tier, not globally: keyfile pulls grant authority to the payor /
  minimizes the standing governor; federation may keep a condition-limited governor.
- The mode enum (keyfile vs federation) belongs in RBRD and is added only when federation lands —
  not in this heat.
- Migration grandfathers existing role-named SAs: recover each citizen's role from the role-prefixed
  SA name (a one-time bootstrap, distinct from the retired standing "derive from IAM"), register
  citizens in the Terrier, let legacy names age out. No flag day.
- The convergence covers operator citizens; cloud-native identities (Mason, future Envoy) share the
  capability-set abstraction but not the citizen lifecycle.

## Retired cinches

- "Roster derives from IAM (an `rbk-identity` label), not a stored list — it cannot drift." Retired:
  under decoupling, deriving from IAM is lossy and makes the intended-vs-actual audit a tautology,
  and SA labels do not exist (SAs support tags, in Preview). Replaced by the Terrier +
  asymmetric audit. The true invariant it reached for — no enforcement state outside IAM — is
  preserved; the false corollary (no intent state may exist) is dropped.

## Open — resolve within the heat

- Where the Terrier physically lives — direction settled: hosted in the **Manor**
  (Payor project), where Terrier-write authority sits. Remaining: the exact object form (a separate
  access-restricted object vs a GAR artifact), explicitly **not** the build bucket (the director
  holds objectCreator there); write-ACL held only by governor/payor (the Terrier-write invariant;
  see The audit).
- Whether the keyfile tier drops the standing governor entirely or demotes it to non-privileged —
  this choice gates whether project-scope grant-limiting is in-heat.
- The citizen orphan marker: SA tags (Preview) vs description sentinel (both advisory; the
  authoritative orphan signal is a key on an SA absent from the Terrier — see memo).
- Whether to offer a combined add+grant convenience wrapper for the common one-hat citizen (the
  two-gesture flow itself follows from the decoupling).

## Sources

rbk-08-credential-repairs; memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races (the lifecycle
split — the enabler); memo-20260527-operator-credential-models (the two-tier plan and cult-verb
shape — breadcrumbed, full update when frozen); memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak
(standalone governor-leak fix); memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model (the mechanism this paddock
points to); memo-20260605-citizen-model-ultracode-process (how this revision was produced);
RBSHR "Operator federation". This revision folds in the 260605 design conversation and two review
passes (single-reviewer + ultracode multi-agent). The 260606 revision adds the Manor↔Census naming,
the first-class RBS0 Manor quoin (`rbtgi_manor`), and the verb dispositions above. The 260609
revision elects **Terrier** for the registry — retiring the C-initial *Census* (it collided with
Crucible) and dropping "ledger" as a competing name.

content.text: `memo-20260609-federation-canon.md` is dated today — that's the one. Reading it plus the citizen-capability mechanism memo.

content.content:
1	# Federation Canon — Operator Workforce-Identity Federation, Final Shape
2	
3	Date: 2026-06-09
4	
5	Status: **CANON.** The single authoritative home for the **federation** operator-credential
6	tier (the paid, organization-required tier — keyless, no operator secret on disk). This document
7	supersedes `memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md` (folded in whole) and **R4** of
8	`memo-20260522-org-affiliated-credential-reorientation.md` (the test-rig refresh-token residual —
9	retired by D2 below).
10	
11	The **citizen / keyfile tier** (the free, no-org tier being *built now* under heat ₣BZ) is **not**
12	restated here — it lives in `memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md` + the ₣BZ paddock. This canon
13	points at it; the two tiers share a capability layer and an accessor seam but never coexist in one
14	depot.
15	
16	---
17	
18	## How to read this
19	
20	Federation lets a depot operator reach GCP **without any secret on disk** — they prove identity by a
21	browser sign-in to their organization's own IdP, and the tool exchanges that proof for a short-lived
22	Google token. It is the heavyweight successor to the keyfile tier, gated by one structural fact: the
23	trust object (a *workforce pool*) is organization-level, so federation requires a GCP org — which is
24	the free/paid line.
25	
26	**If you read only one thing, read "The decisions" below.** The single decision that shapes
27	everything (D2) is that *a human is always present to start a run* — which lets the tier hold **zero
28	durable secrets, anywhere**, shipped or test-rig.
29	
30	Where the keyfile tier lives: `memo-20260605` + ₣BZ paddock. Where the reasoning behind these
31	decisions lives: `memo-20260522` (R1–R5). This canon **states** decisions; it points to the
32	derivation rather than repeating it.
33	
34	---
35	
36	## The decisions (the record)
37	
38	The cinched calls. D2, D4–D6 are this design line's; the rest are carried forward from the source
39	memos and confirmed.
40	
41	- **D1 — Two permanent tiers, one per-depot mode enum.** Keyfile (free, no-org) and federation
42	  (paid, org-required) both ship forever; a depot runs exactly one, selected by a mode enum homed in
43	  RBRD (tamper-evident — see Regime homes). The organization requirement is the paywall.
44	
45	- **D2 — A human is always present to start a run; therefore the federation tier holds NO persisted
46	  refresh token, anywhere.** (Operator decision, 2026-06-09.) The browser sign-in happens at run
47	  kickoff (and again if a run outlives the session cap). This **retires R4's "single acknowledged
48	  durable-secret residual"** — the caged test-rig refresh token is not built. The federation tier
49	  becomes **fully secret-free**: no SA key (it has none by definition), no refresh token, only an
50	  ephemeral in-process access token. *Tradeoff, stated honestly:* truly **unattended scheduled**
51	  federation runs (cron at 3am, nobody present) are **not supported** — they would require the
52	  refresh token D2 declines. Revisit-trigger in Open/deferred.
53	
54	- **D3 — One identity-keyed accessor; downstream model-blind.** Every GCP call resolves a token via a
55	  single accessor keyed by *identity* ("a token as alice"), which branches *inside* by tier. Every
56	  call site adds `Authorization: Bearer <token>` and knows nothing of the token's origin. The
57	  accessor treats its **credential source as opaque and pluggable** — it must not assume a fresh
58	  per-call mint (the one constraint ₣BZ's keyfile-only accessor must honor to keep federation cheap).
59	
60	- **D4 — Regime homes obey the XOR authorship rule.** Every `RBRx.env` is **either** human-edited
61	  **or** infrastructure-maintained — never both. Identity selector → human-edited RBRS field;
62	  durable credential material → full-auto RBRA (keyfile only); mode + federation facts + lifetime
63	  policy → tamper-evident RBRD; ephemeral access token → not a regime at all. (See Regime homes.)
64	
65	- **D5 — Token lifetime is Governor-administered, in tamper-evident RBRD.** Not a per-station human
66	  knob. Bounded by the GCP ceilings (Palisade limits: 1h keyfile, 12h federation) — neither Governor
67	  nor Payor can exceed them. With D2 there is no month-scale "Payor escalation"; long runs that
68	  outlive the cap get a fresh human click.
69	
70	- **D6 — Federation is fully secret-free; the tier contrast is sharp.** Keyfile = exactly **one**
71	  durable secret (the SA key). Federation = **zero**. This is the security payoff of the org
72	  requirement and of D2 together.
73	
74	---
75	
76	## What federation is — the two tiers
77	
78	| | **Keyfile** (free) | **Federation** (paid) |
79	|---|---|---|
80	| Operator secret on disk | SA key (RBRA) | **none** |
81	| GCP organization | not required | **required** |
82	| Identity proof | possession of the key | fresh IdP sign-in (browser) |
83	| Revocation | rotate / delete the key | disable the principal at the IdP |
84	| Capabilities | grants on the citizen's SA | grants on the federated `principal://` subject |
85	| Token mint | signed-JWT exchange | STS token-exchange |
86	| Durable secrets held by RBK | one (the key) | **zero** (D2) |
87	
88	Both tiers terminate in the **same** artifact — a short-lived Google access token — and everything
89	downstream is identical and model-blind. They differ only in identity-kind (citizen ↔ federate) and
90	token mint (signed-JWT ↔ STS).
91	
92	**Why org-only.** Federation's trust object, the **workforce pool**, can only be created at the
93	**organization** level; a plain project cannot hold one. A qualifying org is *free* via Cloud
94	Identity once the owner verifies control of a DNS domain — so the real prerequisite is "the operator
95	controls a domain," not "the operator pays Google." The keyfile tier exists for operators who cannot
96	or will not verify a domain.
97	
98	**Workforce vs Workload — do not conflate.** Federation here is **Workforce** Identity Federation
99	(human identities, org-level pool). It is distinct from the **envoy's** egress federation, which is
100	**Workload** Identity Federation (machine identities, GCP→AWS delivery). Same mechanism family,
101	opposite direction and actor; they never chain for one actor.
102	
103	---
104	
105	## Setup — Payor / org-admin, one-time
106	
107	The Payor, acting as org admin, configures trust once. `gcloud`/REST touches **setup only** — never
108	runtime. (Visual companion: `diagrams/rbdgs_federation-setup.puml`.)
109	
110	1. Create an org-level **workforce pool**.
111	2. Create a **provider** in the pool trusting an external IdP — any compliant **OIDC or SAML 2.0**
112	   issuer (Entra, Okta, AD FS, Ping, Auth0, self-hosted Keycloak/Dex, or GCP Identity Platform as an
113	   in-house IdP).
114	3. Set an **attribute mapping** pinning `google.subject` to an *immutable* IdP claim (`sub`/`oid`,
115	   ≤127 bytes). A *mutable* claim invites spoofing.
116	4. Grant depot capability to the federated principal:
117	   `principal://iam.googleapis.com/locations/global/workforcePools/POOL/subject/SUBJECT`.
118	
119	Identity proofing lives **entirely** in the IdP. RBK grants capability only to an already-known
120	principal; the depot remains the home of the role-to-identity roster (the **Terrier**,
121	in citizen-model terms — see `memo-20260605`). Recording grant *intent* in the Terrier parallels the
122	IAM grant (intent-first); it is **not** consulted at login (see The accessor).
123	
124	---
125	
126	## Login — operator, per session, human present
127	
128	Two legs, both inside RBK's existing curl/openssl/jq stack. (Visual companion:
129	`diagrams/rbdgl_federation-login.puml`.)
130	
131	**Leg 1 — IdP auth, the OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant (RFC 8628).** Headless-friendly: the
132	browser need not be on the box.
133	
134	1. The tool POSTs the IdP's device-authorization endpoint → `user_code`, `verification_uri`,
135	   `device_code`, `interval`.
136	2. The tool prints **"go to `<uri>`, enter `WXYZ-1234`."** The human authorizes in **any** browser,
137	   on any device, proving themselves to *their own* IdP. **Google is not involved in Leg 1.**
138	3. The tool polls the IdP's token endpoint until the human approves → returns the **OIDC ID token
139	   (JWT)**.
140	
141	   - **Clickable / QR upgrade.** Where the IdP returns the optional `verification_uri_complete`
142	     (the URI with the `user_code` embedded — Entra/Okta/Auth0/Keycloak/Zitadel all do), the tool may
143	     print a clickable link or QR instead of "enter this code." This removes the *typing*, not the
144	     *approval*.
145	   - **The human approval is load-bearing and cannot be encoded away.** Even with the complete URI,
146	     RFC 8628 still has the server confirm the `user_code` matches the device (anti-phishing), and
147	     the approval *is* the identity proof — it is what replaces "possession of the key." Removing it
148	     would turn federation back into a stored-credential model.
149	   - **No `offline_access` scope is requested** (D2) — so the IdP returns **no refresh token**.
150	
151	**Leg 2 — STS exchange (pure REST against `sts.googleapis.com`, RFC 8693):**
152	
153	```
154	POST https://sts.googleapis.com/v1/token        # send NO Authorization header
155	  grant_type         = urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:token-exchange
156	  audience           = //iam.googleapis.com/locations/global/workforcePools/POOL/providers/PROVIDER
157	  subject_token      = <IdP ID token from Leg 1>
158	  subject_token_type = urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:id_token
159	  → short-lived Google access_token
160	```
161	
162	Leg 2 has no signing — it POSTs a token RBK already holds. Then bearer the access token against any
163	GCP API.
164	
165	**Session cap.** A Workforce session is configurable **15 min – 12 h, default 1 h** — a hard GCP
166	ceiling. A run that outlives its session does **not** extend the token; the human re-clicks Leg 1.
167	Under D2 there is no headless re-mint, by design.
168	
169	---
170	
171	## The Claude Code orchestration pattern (secret-free kickoff)
172	
173	A non-trusted orchestrator (e.g. a Claude Code session driving a test run) can start an
174	**authenticated** run without ever holding a secret:
175	
176	- The orchestrator runs the tabtarget that initiates Leg 1 and surfaces only the **`user_code` +
177	  `verification_uri`** — neither is a secret (single-use, useless without the human's own IdP auth).
178	- The **human clicks in their own browser** and approves. Nothing is pasted into the orchestrator.
179	- The resulting **access token lives inside the spawned tabtarget process**, not in the orchestrator's
180	  conversation context.
181	
182	So the operator's mental model holds: *a Claude Code session kicks off an authenticated run on one
183	secret-free human click.* This is the device flow's central virtue — the requesting agent is
184	decoupled from the authenticating browser.
185	
186	---
187	
188	## The accessor — the single seam
189	
190	(Visual companion: `diagrams/rbdgm_federation-seam.puml`.) One function resolves "give me a token as
191	`<identity>`," branching inside by the depot's mode:
192	
193	- **keyfile** → resolve identity → RBRA key → **signed-JWT** exchange → access token.
194	- **federation** → device flow (Leg 1) → STS exchange (Leg 2) → access token.
195	
196	Downstream is already model-blind. The branch point is the accessor's **credential source**, which is
197	*pluggable* (D3): for federation it drives the device flow; for keyfile it mints from the key. The
198	accessor must never assume a per-call mint — it caches the live session's token (≤ session cap) so
199	the many tabtargets of one run reuse it without re-prompting. **It must fail loud, never silently
200	prompt, if no live session exists in a context where a human cannot click** (the headless
201	fail-fast membrane).
202	
203	---
204	
205	## Regime homes & the XOR authorship rule (D4)
206	
207	What a workstation holds, decomposed so each file is *either* human-edited *or* infra-maintained:
208	
209	| What | Home | Authorship | Notes |
210	|---|---|---|---|
211	| **Identity selector** ("this home is alice") | RBRS field (`RBRS_CITIZEN`) | **human** | inert, singular per home (one home = one operator), stable |
212	| **Durable credential material** | RBRA | **infra (full-auto)** | *keyfile only.* Delivered by add/rekey; operator never edits. Federation has none. |
213	| **Mode enum + federation facts** (pool, provider, audience) + **lifetime policy** | RBRD | **infra (Payor/Governor-inscribed)** | tripwire-protected ⇒ tamper-evident; one mode per depot |
214	| **Ephemeral access token** | — | — | **not a regime** — runtime scratch (process/keychain, ≤ session cap) |
215	
216	Consequences worth stating:
217	
218	- **RBRA goes full-auto** in the citizen model — its sole authors are the `add`/`rekey` verbs. Any
219	  human-tunable that rides it today (the `RBRA_TOKEN_LIFETIME_SEC` knob is the suspect) **must be
220	  evicted** to keep RBRA cleanly infra-owned — and its new home is the Governor-administered lifetime
221	  policy in RBRD (D5), not a station field. The XOR rule *forces* this eviction.
222	- **Shared workstation is out of scope by operator rule:** distinct operators must have distinct
223	  homes/filesystems. So a regime's scope (a home) is single-citizen, and RBRA is a true per-home
224	  singleton — the role-indexed manifold of today collapses to one.
225	
226	---
227	
228	## Token lifetime policy (D5)
229	
230	Lifetime is **Governor-administered** and lives in tamper-evident **RBRD**, so a compromised station
231	cannot widen its own token window. It is bounded by GCP's hard ceilings — **1 h** (keyfile signed-JWT)
232	and **12 h** (federation Workforce session) — which are Palisade limits no role can exceed.
233	
234	- **Where it earns its keep:** in *federation* the access-token window is the *only* exposure, so the
235	  knob is load-bearing. In *keyfile* the SA key on disk dwarfs the token as the real risk, so a tight
236	  TTL buys little — implement it for uniformity (via RBRD, honored at mint), but do not over-invest.
237	- **No month-scale escalation:** under D2 there is no refresh token, so the earlier-explored
238	  "special Payor operation to greatly extend lifetime for CI/CD" is **retired**. Long runs get a human
239	  re-click, not a longer token.
240	- *(Deferred refinement, not built:* lifetime keyed by **capability-set** — a low-blast-radius
241	  retriever token allowed a longer window than a high-blast-radius governor token. Ties to the
242	  blast-radius cinch; hold, don't build.*)*
243	
244	---
245	
246	## Relationship to the citizen (keyfile) tier
247	
248	Federation is the future **swap**, not a rewrite: the citizen model's capability layer
249	(capability-sets, grant/revoke, the Terrier, the audit) is built **tier-blind**, so the
250	federation switch reshapes nothing in it — it changes only **identity-kind** (citizen → *federate*)
251	and **token mint** (signed-JWT → STS). Identity verbs branch by tier; capability verbs do not. The
252	*federate* identity is owned by the IdP (no SA minted, no key, no rekey — the IdP owns rotation;
253	remove = de-register + revoke-all). Full mechanism: `memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md` + ₣BZ
254	paddock.
255	
256	---
257	
258	## What this supersedes / points to (the map)
259	
260	| Document | Disposition |
261	|---|---|
262	| `memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md` | **Folded here in whole.** Now a superseded-banner pointer. |
263	| `memo-20260522` **R4** (test-rig refresh token) | **Superseded by D2** (no refresh token; fully secret-free). |
264	| `memo-20260522` R1–R3, R5 + §7–§8 | **Derivation of record** — the human-vs-unattended axis and resolutions. Point-to, don't restate. |
265	| `memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md` + ₣BZ paddock | **The citizen/keyfile tier — the active build.** Separate timeline; not absorbed here. |
266	| `memo-20260427-google-native-human-auth.md` | Impersonation mechanism detail; its human-auth *framing* was already superseded (20260522 R2). |
267	| RBSHR "Operator federation" roadmap entry | This canon is its detail; the entry now points here. |
268	
269	---
270	
271	## Open / deferred (with revisit-triggers)
272	
273	- **Unattended scheduled federation runs** — *deliberately unsupported* per D2 (no refresh token).
274	  *Revisit-trigger:* a concrete need for cron-fired federation CI with no human present; reintroducing
275	  R4's caged, firewall-bound, centrally-revocable refresh token is the known path, to be re-decided
276	  then.
277	- **The federation-setup verb's exact API calls** (pool/provider/attribute-mapping creation) and the
278	  chosen IdP(s) for the project's own test org.
279	- **Concrete credential-source wiring** for the interactive human (device-flow output → what the
280	  accessor's token resolver consumes) and the headless **fail-fast** behavior when no human can click.
281	- **RBRD field names** for the mode enum, federation facts, and the Governor lifetime policy.
282	- **Diagram Source: lines** — `diagrams/rbdg{l,s,k,m}_*.puml` still cite `memo-20260527`; repoint to
283	  this canon and re-render on the next pluml fixture run (container work, deferred from this edit).
284	
285	---
286	
287	## Sources
288	
289	- `memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md` (folded), `memo-20260522-org-affiliated-credential-reorientation.md` (R1–R5 derivation), `memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md` (citizen tier).
290	- Diagrams: `diagrams/rbdgl_federation-login.puml`, `rbdgs_federation-setup.puml`, `rbdgk_keyfile-login.puml`, `rbdgm_federation-seam.puml`.
291	- [Workforce Identity Federation — overview](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-identity-federation) · [Configure Workforce Identity Federation](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/configuring-workforce-identity-federation) · [Obtain short-lived tokens](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-obtaining-short-lived-credentials).
292	- [RFC 8628 — Device Authorization Grant](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8628) · [RFC 8693 — Token Exchange](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8693).
293	- [Cloud Identity editions](https://docs.cloud.google.com/identity/docs/editions) (free org via domain verification).
